stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the GP with 6 months of hard infrequent stools every 4 to 5 days and recent daily faecal soiling of his underwear. He has crampy abdominal pain that improves after stooling. He passed meconium at 18 hours of age, growth percentiles are appropriate for age and the lumbosacral spine is normal. He has been on lactulose 5 mL twice daily for 8 weeks without improvement.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft with palpable suprapubic faecal mass; perianal inspection normal with no fissure or anteriorly displaced anus; normal lower limb tone, reflexes and gait
data_table.Investigations: Growth chart: height and weight tracking 50th centile; no plain abdominal x-ray performed

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence outpatient high-dose macrogol 3350 with electrolytes disimpaction regimen over 3 days
options.rationale: Functional constipation with overflow soiling and palpable faecal mass is managed with disimpaction followed by maintenance laxatives; high-dose macrogol with electrolytes outpatient over 3 days is first-line per RCH and NICE. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Refer to paediatric surgery for assessment for Hirschsprung disease and anorectal manometry
options.rationale: Hirschsprung disease is unlikely given timely meconium passage, appropriate growth and absence of red flags; surgical referral is not indicated as the initial step. (Source: NICE CG99: Constipation in children and young people)
source_refs: NICE CG99: Constipation in children and young people

options.text: Admit for nasogastric polyethylene glycol lavage given outpatient laxative failure to date
options.rationale: Inpatient nasogastric PEG lavage is reserved for failure of outpatient disimpaction; an adequate disimpaction regimen has not yet been trialled. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Add a daily sodium picosulfate stimulant to current lactulose for ongoing chronic management
options.rationale: Adding a stimulant without first disimpacting the rectal faecal mass will not relieve overflow soiling and risks ongoing cramping; disimpaction must precede maintenance therapy. (Source: NICE CG99: Constipation in children and young people)
source_refs: NICE CG99: Constipation in children and young people

options.text: Order plain abdominal x-ray to confirm faecal loading before changing management today
options.rationale: Plain abdominal x-ray is not routinely required when faecal loading is clinically evident and adds radiation without changing management. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

explanation.summary: Functional constipation with overflow soiling in a child without red flags is managed with outpatient disimpaction using high-dose macrogol with electrolytes over 3 days, followed by maintenance laxatives and behavioural measures. Inpatient lavage is reserved for outpatient failure. Surgical referral and routine imaging are not indicated when red flags are absent.
key_points: Disimpaction precedes maintenance therapy in functional constipation with overflow
High-dose macrogol with electrolytes over 3 days is the standard outpatient regimen
Red flag features prompt referral but routine imaging is not required
explanation.pearls: Educate that maintenance laxatives are continued for at least 6 months, with toileting routines after meals to use the gastrocolic reflex; weaning is gradual after several months of regular soft stools.

sources.label: NICE CG99: Constipation in children and young people

subtopic_detail: Infantile spasms recognition and initial therapy
stem: A previously well 5-month-old girl is brought to the emergency department with a 2-week history of recurrent brief episodes of sudden symmetric flexion of her head, arms and legs, occurring in clusters of 10 to 30 events shortly after waking. Her parents have captured several events on video. Over the same period her parents report loss of social smiling and reduced engagement compared with her baseline. She is afebrile and is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Examination: Alert but quiet, no focal neurology, anterior fontanelle soft, head circumference 50th centile; no neurocutaneous stigmata on inspection of skin
data_table.Investigations: Capillary glucose 4.8; videos reviewed show characteristic flexor spasms in clusters with brief crying after each cluster

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure parents that these are benign myoclonic jerks of infancy and require no investigation
options.rationale: Loss of developmental skills with clustered flexor spasms is highly suggestive of infantile epileptic spasms syndrome rather than benign myoclonus of infancy and requires urgent assessment. (Source: RCH CPG: Infantile spasms)

options.text: Arrange urgent neurology referral and EEG within 24 to 48 hours and start oral prednisolone 40 mg daily
options.rationale: Infantile epileptic spasms syndrome requires urgent EEG looking for hypsarrhythmia and prompt first-line therapy with prednisolone or vigabatrin (vigabatrin if tuberous sclerosis); delayed treatment worsens developmental outcome. (Source: Royal Australian College of Physicians: Infantile epileptic spasms syndrome)
source_refs: Royal Australian College of Physicians: Infantile epileptic spasms syndrome

options.text: Trial oral phenobarbitone 5 mg/kg daily and review response in clinic at 4 weeks
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone is not effective for infantile spasms; first-line therapy is hormonal (prednisolone or ACTH) or vigabatrin, with rapid review for response. (Source: RCH CPG: Infantile spasms)

options.text: Order outpatient MRI brain at 4 weeks and review with results before treatment
options.rationale: Treatment must not be delayed for MRI; commencing first-line therapy promptly while imaging is arranged optimises developmental outcome. (Source: RCH CPG: Infantile spasms)

options.text: Commence sodium valproate 20 mg/kg daily and review in 2 weeks for clinical response
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is not first-line for infantile spasms; hormonal therapy and vigabatrin have higher response rates and faster resolution of hypsarrhythmia. (Source: Royal Australian College of Physicians: Infantile epileptic spasms syndrome)
source_refs: Royal Australian College of Physicians: Infantile epileptic spasms syndrome

explanation.summary: Infantile epileptic spasms syndrome (West syndrome) presents with clusters of flexor spasms, developmental regression and hypsarrhythmia on EEG. Prompt first-line treatment with hormonal therapy (prednisolone or ACTH) or vigabatrin within days improves developmental outcome. Sodium valproate and phenobarbitone are not first-line. Treatment is not delayed for MRI.
key_points: Clustered flexor spasms with developmental regression is the West syndrome triad
First-line therapy is prednisolone or ACTH; vigabatrin if tuberous sclerosis
Delay to treatment worsens neurodevelopmental outcome
explanation.pearls: Look for ash-leaf macules and other neurocutaneous stigmata; tuberous sclerosis with infantile spasms is preferentially treated with vigabatrin.

sources.label: Royal Australian College of Physicians: Infantile epileptic spasms syndrome

subtopic_detail: Neonatal HSV central nervous system disease
stem: An 18-day-old term neonate is brought to the emergency department with 24 hours of lethargy, poor feeding, fever to 38.4 degrees Celsius and clusters of vesicles on the scalp at the site of a fetal scalp electrode. The mother had primary genital herpes at 36 weeks of gestation and was on suppressive aciclovir from 36 weeks. He was born by spontaneous vaginal birth.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.4 degrees Celsius, HR 180, RR 60, SpO2 96% in room air, capillary glucose 3.6
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, decreased tone, anterior fontanelle full but not bulging, scalp vesicles in clusters on a red base; chest clear; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Blood culture pending; CSF: WCC 50 (mononuclear), protein 1.2 g/L, glucose 2.4; CSF HSV-2 PCR positive; surface viral swabs pending

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: IV cefotaxime 50 mg/kg eight-hourly for 14 days as empirical bacterial meningitis cover only
options.rationale: Empirical bacterial cover does not treat HSV; in any neonate with CSF HSV PCR positive disease, IV aciclovir is mandatory and prolonged. (Source: ASID: Neonatal herpes simplex virus infection)
source_refs: ASID: Neonatal herpes simplex virus infection

options.text: Oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg three times daily for 14 days as outpatient management with review
options.rationale: Oral aciclovir has inadequate bioavailability and CNS penetration in neonatal HSV; IV therapy for 21 days is required for disseminated or CNS disease. (Source: ASID: Neonatal herpes simplex virus infection)
source_refs: ASID: Neonatal herpes simplex virus infection

options.text: IV aciclovir 20 mg/kg eight-hourly for 21 days with ophthalmology and neurology review
options.rationale: Neonatal HSV with CNS or disseminated involvement is treated with IV aciclovir 20 mg/kg/dose every 8 hours for 21 days, with ophthalmology assessment and a repeat CSF PCR at the end of therapy to confirm clearance. (Source: ASID: Neonatal herpes simplex virus infection)
source_refs: ASID: Neonatal herpes simplex virus infection

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily and IV amoxicillin 50 mg/kg six-hourly for 10 days
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is contraindicated in neonates due to bilirubin displacement and calcium precipitation; HSV CNS disease additionally requires IV aciclovir. (Source: RCH CPG: Herpes simplex virus)

options.text: IV ganciclovir 6 mg/kg twice daily for 21 days targeting cytomegalovirus disease
options.rationale: Ganciclovir is reserved for congenital CMV disease; it is not the agent for HSV-2 neonatal CNS infection where aciclovir is the standard. (Source: ASID: Neonatal herpes simplex virus infection)
source_refs: ASID: Neonatal herpes simplex virus infection

explanation.summary: Neonatal HSV with CNS or disseminated disease requires IV aciclovir 20 mg/kg every 8 hours for 21 days, with end-of-therapy repeat CSF PCR and ophthalmology review. Oral aciclovir is inadequate as initial therapy. Ceftriaxone is contraindicated in neonates. After completion of IV therapy, suppressive oral aciclovir is given for 6 months.
key_points: Neonatal HSV CNS disease needs IV aciclovir 20 mg/kg q8h for 21 days
Oral aciclovir is inadequate as primary therapy for neonatal HSV
Ceftriaxone is contraindicated in neonates due to bilirubin displacement
explanation.pearls: After IV therapy, neonates receive suppressive oral aciclovir 300 mg/m2 three times daily for 6 months to reduce neurodevelopmental morbidity and recurrent skin disease.

sources.label: ASID: Neonatal herpes simplex virus infection

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 at 14 weeks of gestation presents to her general practitioner with 1 week of grey malodorous vaginal discharge and mild vulval irritation. She has no fever, abdominal pain or bleeding. Her first pregnancy was uncomplicated with a term vaginal birth. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. She has no allergies and is taking pregnancy multivitamins only.

data_table.Examination: Speculum: thin homogeneous grey-white discharge coating vaginal walls; cervix appears healthy with no friability; uterus appropriate size for gestation
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH greater than 4.5; wet mount: clue cells present; whiff test positive; NAAT for chlamydia and gonorrhoea negative

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate treatment?

options.text: Oral fluconazole 150 mg as a single dose targeting vulvovaginal candidiasis as cause
options.rationale: Fluconazole treats vulvovaginal candidiasis; the clinical picture and Amsel criteria are diagnostic of bacterial vaginosis rather than candida. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Bacterial vaginosis)

options.text: Intravaginal clindamycin 2% cream once daily at night for 7 nights as topical therapy
options.rationale: Intravaginal clindamycin cream has been associated with later membrane rupture and is generally avoided in the second half of pregnancy in Australian guidance. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Bacterial vaginosis)

options.text: Reassure that bacterial vaginosis does not require treatment in low-risk pregnancy currently
options.rationale: Symptomatic bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy is treated to relieve symptoms and may reduce upper genital tract infection; observation alone is inappropriate when symptomatic. (Source: RANZCOG: Routine antenatal assessment)

options.text: Oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 7 days as systemic antibiotic therapy treatment
options.rationale: Oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 7 days is the Australian Therapeutic Guidelines first-line regimen for symptomatic bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy at any gestation. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Bacterial vaginosis)

options.text: Intravaginal metronidazole gel 0.75% once daily at night for 5 nights as topical therapy
options.rationale: Topical metronidazole gel is less preferred than oral therapy in symptomatic pregnant women in Australian guidance; oral therapy treats potential subclinical upper tract carriage. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Bacterial vaginosis)

explanation.summary: Symptomatic bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy is treated with oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 7 days. Intravaginal clindamycin cream is avoided in later pregnancy due to possible association with membrane rupture. Observation is inappropriate when symptomatic; fluconazole and antibiotics with no vaginal coverage are not relevant.
key_points: Oral metronidazole 400 mg BD for 7 days is first-line for symptomatic BV in pregnancy
Intravaginal clindamycin cream is avoided in the second half of pregnancy
Amsel criteria diagnose BV: thin discharge, pH greater than 4.5, clue cells, positive whiff
explanation.pearls: There is no role for routine screening and treatment of asymptomatic bacterial vaginosis in low-risk pregnancies; screening and treatment is reserved for symptomatic women.

stem: A 32-year-old G1 at 26 weeks of gestation attends her midwife appointment with the results of her routine 75 g oral glucose tolerance test. She is asymptomatic and has had an uncomplicated pregnancy to date. She has no family history of diabetes. Her booking BMI was 27. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander and has no other risk factors.

data_table.Examination: Symphysis-fundal height appropriate for gestation, fetal heart 145 beats per minute, no oedema; BP 118/72
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT (mmol/L): fasting 5.4, 1 hour 10.6, 2 hour 8.8 (ADIPS thresholds 5.1 / 10.0 / 8.5); HbA1c 5.4%

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg twice daily titrating to a maximum of 2 g daily over 2 weeks
options.rationale: Metformin is added if glucose targets are not met on lifestyle measures alone over 1 to 2 weeks; initial therapy is medical nutrition therapy with monitoring. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational diabetes consensus)

options.text: Refer to obstetrician for insulin initiation given multiple abnormal OGTT values today
options.rationale: Insulin initiation at diagnosis is reserved for marked hyperglycaemia or maternal factors; mild OGTT abnormalities warrant a trial of lifestyle first per ADIPS. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational diabetes consensus)

options.text: Repeat the 75 g OGTT in 4 weeks for confirmation before initiating any management
options.rationale: A single abnormal value on the diagnostic OGTT establishes gestational diabetes; repeating the test delays appropriate care. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis of gestational diabetes mellitus)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis of gestational diabetes mellitus

options.text: Defer treatment until 32 weeks to allow physiological glucose changes to stabilise first
options.rationale: Earlier intervention reduces macrosomia and shoulder dystocia; deferring treatment is not appropriate once GDM is diagnosed. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational diabetes consensus)

options.text: Initiate medical nutrition therapy, exercise advice and home glucose monitoring with review in 1-2 weeks
options.rationale: ADIPS recommends initial management of GDM with medical nutrition therapy, exercise advice and self monitoring of blood glucose; pharmacotherapy is added when targets are not achieved within 1 to 2 weeks. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational diabetes consensus)

explanation.summary: Initial management of newly diagnosed gestational diabetes is medical nutrition therapy with exercise and home glucose monitoring, with review in 1 to 2 weeks. Pharmacotherapy with metformin or insulin is added when glycaemic targets are not met. A single abnormal value on the diagnostic OGTT establishes the diagnosis and treatment should not be deferred.
key_points: GDM is diagnosed on a single abnormal OGTT value per ADIPS
Initial therapy is lifestyle with home glucose monitoring and 1-2 week review
Pharmacotherapy starts if fasting or postprandial targets are not met
explanation.pearls: Australian targets are fasting capillary glucose less than 5.0 mmol/L and 1 hour postprandial less than 7.4 mmol/L; persistently exceeding either prompts the addition of metformin or insulin.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Diagnosis of gestational diabetes mellitus
